Long ago, before clocks counted time, the river spoke to those who listened, and the hills kept watch over dreaming fields. In a village where reeds bent like tired singers, a fisherman named Rami prayed to the water at dawn. He did not worship the river so much as command it, casting nets with a surgeon’s certainty and keeping the best catches for himself, never sharing with neighbors who toiled beside him.

One spring the river swallowed his pride and swelled with a voice that sounded like voices at once: child, mother, grandchild, river spirit. It offered abundance if Rami would tell the truth about his catches and share half of his bounty. Rami laughed and tossed a handful of coins toward the current, promising to give away a portion later, when the fish were few and the river needed appeasement.

The river rose higher, spilling silver foam into the lanes, until Rami could not fish without breaking his nets. He began to understand that the river did not belong to him, only through him. So he returned to the bank, shared his next haul with the village, and spoke aloud the honest accounting of every fish.

The river listened.

Aira promised truth, and Lumin revived. The river shone; the village blessed.

He believed a fair share would heal both land and kin, yet his ledger grew heavy with regret.

When the ledger finally opened itself, the figures shone: a portion saved for cold winters, a portion shared with the potter who mumbled over clay, and a portion cast back to the river so its current could bring life downstream.

The river accepted the accounting with a soft sigh, and the village woke to a season of enough. Together, always.
